q: Three particles start from origin at the same time, one with velocity $v_1$ along positive x-axis, the second along the positive y-axis with a velocity $v_2$ and the third along the line $y = x$ with such a speed that all the three always stay in a straight line. Then the velocity of the third particles is
question: Three particles start from origin at the same time, one with velocity $v_1$ along positive x-axis, the second along the positive y-axis with a velocity $v_2$ and the third along the line $y = x$ with such a speed that all the three always stay in a straight line. Then the velocity of the third particles is
solution:
After a time $t$, let the positions of the three particles be A, B, and C.

Since particle 1 moves along the x-axis with velocity $v_1$, its coordinates is A = $(v_1 t, 0)$.

Particle 2 moves along the y-axis with velocity $v_2$, its coordinates are $(0, v_2 t)$.

The particle 3 moves along the line $y = x$.

Let its velocity be $v_3$. Its distance from the origin is $v_3 t$.

$$(v_3 t \, \cos 45^{\circ}, \, v_3 t \, \sin 45^{\circ}) = \left(\frac{v_3 t}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{v_3 t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

If three points $(x_1, y_1)$, $(x_2, y_2)$, and $(x_3, y_3)$ are collinear the slope between any two pairs must be the same.

For the equation of the line passing through A$(v_1 t, 0)$ and B$(0, v_2 t)$ using the intercept form:

$$\frac{x}{\text{x-intercept}} + \frac{y}{\text{y-intercept}} = 1$$

$$\frac{x}{v_1 t} + \frac{y}{v_2 t} = 1$$

Since Particle 3 always stays on this line, its coordinates $\left(\frac{v_3 t}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{v_3 t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ must satisfy the equation:

$$\frac{(v_3 t/\sqrt{2})}{v_1 t} + \frac{(v_3 t/\sqrt{2})}{v_2 t} = 1$$

$$\frac{v_3}{\sqrt{2}v_1} + \frac{v_3}{\sqrt{2}v_2} = 1$$

$$\frac{v_3}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{1}{v_1} + \frac{1}{v_2}\right) = 1$$

$$\frac{v_3}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{v_1\,v_2}\right) = 1$$

$$v_3 = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,v_1\,v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the third particle is $\frac{\sqrt{2}\,v_1\,v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$. Hence, the correct option is (D).

q: The trajectory of a particle is as shown in the fig. and its trajectory follows the equation $y = (x-1)^3 + 1$. Find co-ordinates of the point A on the curve such that the direction of instantaneous velocity at A is same as direction of average velocity for the motion O to A.
question: The trajectory of a particle is as shown in the fig. and its trajectory follows the equation $y = (x-1)^3 + 1$. Find co-ordinates of the point A on the curve such that the direction of instantaneous velocity at A is same as direction of average velocity for the motion O to A.
solution:
For a particle moving along a curve $y = f(x)$, the direction of its instantaneous velocity at any point is given by the slope of the tangent to the curve at that point.

$$\text{Slope (tangent)} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

The given trajectory is $y = (x - 1)^3 + 1$. To find the slope of the tangent at any point $(x,y)$,

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x - 1)^3 + 1\right]$$

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 3(x - 1)^2 \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(x - 1) + 0$$

$$\text{Slope of tangent} = 3(x - 1)^2 \ldots (i)$$

The average velocity for motion from the origin O$(0,0)$ to a point A$(x,y)$ is directed along the chord connecting those two points. The slope of this chord represents the direction.

$$\text{Slope (chord)} = \frac{y_{\text{final}} - y_{\text{initial}}}{x_{\text{final}} - x_{\text{initial}}} = \frac{y - 0}{x - 0} = \frac{y}{x}$$

The average velocity from O$(0,0)$ to A$(x,y)$ is,

slope (Chord) = $$\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y-0}{x-0} = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{(x-1)^3+1}{x}$$

slope (Chord) = $$\frac{(x-1)^3+1}{x}$$

If the directions are the same, the slope of the tangent at point A must equal the slope of the chord OA.

$$3(x-1)^2 = \frac{(x-1)^3+1}{x}$$

$$3x(x-1)^2 = (x-1)^3+1$$

$$3x(x^2-2x+1) = [x^3-3x^2+3x-1]+1$$

$$3x^3-6x^2+3x = x^3-3x^2+3x$$

$$3x^3-6x^2 = x^3-3x^2$$

$$2x^3-3x^2 = 0$$

$$x^2(2x-3) = 0$$

Since point A is not at the origin, $x \ne 0$. Therefore:

$$2x-3=0 \rightarrow x=\frac{3}{2}$$

Substituting $x=\frac{3}{2}$ into the trajectory equation:

$$y=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)^3+1$$

$$y=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3+1 = \frac{1}{8}+1$$

$$y=\frac{9}{8}$$

Therefore, the coordinates of point A are $\left(\frac{3}{2},\frac{9}{8}\right)$. Hence, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A rod AB is shown in figure. End A of the rod is fixed on the ground. Block is moving with velocity $2\ \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ towards right. The vertical component of velocity of end B of rod at the instant shown in figure is
question: A rod AB is shown in figure. End A of the rod is fixed on the ground. Block is moving with velocity $2\ \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ towards right. The vertical component of velocity of end B of rod at the instant shown in figure is
solution:
Point A is fixed at the origin on the horizontal ground $(\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{y}=0)$.

Point B is moving tip of the rod, which rests against the vertical face of the block.

Let the instantaneous coordinates of point B be $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$.

Let L be the total length of the rigid rod AB. Since the rod is rigid, L remains completely constant

$$x = L\cos\theta$$

$$y = L\sin\theta$$

At the specific instant the angle of the rod with the horizontal ground is given as $\theta = 30^{\circ}$.

Velocity is the rate of change of position components with respect to time $(t)$.

The block moves towards the right at a constant speed of $2\ \text{m/s}$. Since point B is constrained to stay in contact with the moving vertical face of this block, the horizontal position $x$ of point B is increasing at this rate:

$$v_x = \frac{dx}{dt} = 2\ \text{m/s}$$

The vertical component of the velocity of end B $(v_y)$ is

$$v_y = \frac{dy}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(L\cos\theta) = -L\sin\theta \times \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

$$v_x = -L\sin\theta \times \frac{d\theta}{dt} \ldots (i)$$

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(L\sin\theta) = L\cos\theta \times \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

$$v_y = L\cos\theta \times \frac{d\theta}{dt} \ldots (ii)$$

On dividing equation (ii) by equation (i):

$$\frac{v_y}{v_x} = \frac{L\cos\theta\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}}{-L\sin\theta\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}}$$

$$\frac{v_y}{v_x} = -\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = -\cot\theta$$

$$\left|\frac{v_y}{v_x}\right| = \cot\theta \Rightarrow v_y = v_x\cot\theta$$

Substituting the given values $(v_x = 2\ \text{m/s}\text{ and }\theta = 30^{\circ})$

$$v_y = 2 \times \cot(30^{\circ})$$

$$v_y = 2\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the vertical component of the velocity of end B of the rod is $2\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

q: A ball is projected from a certain point on the surface of a planet at a certain angle with the horizontal surface. The horizontal and vertical displacement x and y varies with time t in second as: $x = 10\sqrt{3}t$ and $y = 10t - t^2$ The maximum height attained by the ball is
question: A ball is projected from a certain point on the surface of a planet at a certain angle with the horizontal surface. The horizontal and vertical displacement x and y varies with time t in second as: $x = 10\sqrt{3}t$ and $y = 10t - t^2$ The maximum height attained by the ball is
solution:
Perpendicular components along the horizontal axis (x) and vertical axis (y) as functions of time $t$:

$$x(t) = 10\sqrt{3}\,t$$

$$y(t) = 10t - t^2$$

The instantaneous velocity component along any direction is the first derivative of its displacement with respect to time

The vertical position function $y(t)$ to find the vertical velocity function $V_y(t)$:

$$V_y = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(10t - t^2)$$

Thus, the vertical velocity at any time $t$ is $V_y(t) = 10 - 2t$

As the ball travels upwards, its vertical velocity decreases continuously until it reaches its highest peak. At this turning point (maximum height), the upward motion stops momentarily before the ball begins to descend.

Therefore, the condition at maximum height is $v_y = 0$

$$10 - 2t_{\text{peak}} = 0$$

$$2t_{\text{peak}} = 10$$

$$t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\ \text{s}$$

To calculate the peak vertical displacement ($y_{\max}$), substituting the value $t_{\text{peak}} = 5\ \text{s}$ into the vertical position equation $y(t)$:

$$y_{\max} = 10(5) - (5)^2$$

$$y_{\max} = 50 - 25$$

$$y_{\max} = 25\ \text{m}$$

Therefore, the maximum height attained by the ball is $25\ \text{m}$. Thus, the correct option is D.

subject: Physics
q: On an inclined plane of inclination $30^{\circ}$, a ball is thrown at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal from the foot of the incline with a velocity of $10\sqrt{3}\,\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$. If $g=10\,\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$, then the time in which the ball will hit the inclined plane is –
question: On an inclined plane of inclination $30^{\circ}$, a ball is thrown at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal from the foot of the incline with a velocity of $10\sqrt{3}\,\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$. If $g=10\,\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$, then the time in which the ball will hit the inclined plane is –
solution:
Let the $x'$-axis lie parallel to and along the surface of the inclined plane, pointing upwards and the $y'$-axis be perpendicular to the surface of the inclined plane, pointing outwards.

The angle of projection relative to the incline surface is:

$$\theta' = 60^\circ - 30^\circ = 30^\circ$$

Initial velocity perpendicular to the incline is,

$$u_{y'} = u \sin(\theta') = 10\sqrt{3}\sin(30^\circ)$$

$$u_{y'} = 10\sqrt{3} \times \frac{1}{2} = 5\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$$

The gravitational acceleration vector $g$ acts straight downwards.

The component acting perpendicular to and directly into the face of the incline is:

$$a_{y'} = -g\cos(30^\circ)$$

Given $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and $\cos(30^\circ) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$a_{y'} = -10 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -5\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}^2$$

As the ball travels upwards, its vertical velocity decreases continuously until it reaches its highest peak. At this turning point (maximum height), the upward motion stops momentarily before the ball begins to descend.

Therefore, the condition at maximum height is $v_y = 0$

$$10 - 2t_{\text{peak}} = 0$$

$$2t_{\text{peak}} = 10$$

$$t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\ \text{s}$$

To calculate the peak vertical displacement ($y_{\max}$), substituting the value $t_{\text{peak}} = 5\ \text{s}$ into the vertical position equation $y(t)$:

$$y_{\max} = 10(5) - (5)^2$$

$$y_{\max} = 50 - 25$$

$$y_{\max} = 25\ \text{m}$$

Therefore, the maximum height attained by the ball is $25\ \text{m}$. Thus, the correct option is D.

q: Two particles are moving with velocities v1 and v2. Their relative velocity is the maximum, when the angle between their velocities is :
question: Two particles are moving with velocities v1 and v2. Their relative velocity is the maximum, when the angle between their velocities is :
solution:
Let two particles, $P_1$ and $P_2$, move with velocity vectors $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$ respectively.

Let the angle between these two vectors be $\theta$.

The relative velocity of particle 1 with respect to particle 2 is,

$$\vec{v}_{1/2} = \vec{v}_1 - \vec{v}_2$$

The magnitude of this relative velocity vector,

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 - 2v_1\,v_2\cos\theta}$$

To maximize the relative velocity $v_{\mathrm{rel}}$ for two given particle speeds $v_1$ and $v_2$,

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} \Rightarrow \text{Minimize } \cos\theta$$

The cosine function is between a maximum value of $+1$ and a minimum value of $-1$. Thus:

$$\cos\theta_{\mathrm{minimum}} = -1$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(-1) = \pi \text{ radians}$$

Therefore, the relative velocity reaches its maximum magnitude when the angle between the two velocity vectors is $\pi$. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: Three stones A, B, C are projected from surface of very long inclined plane with equal speeds and different angles of projection as shown in figure. The incline makes an angle $\theta$ with horizontal. If $H_A$, $H_B$ and $H_C$ are maximum height attained by A, B and C respectively above inclined plane then: (Neglect air friction)
question: Three stones A, B, C are projected from surface of very long inclined plane with equal speeds and different angles of projection as shown in figure. The incline makes an angle $\theta$ with horizontal. If $H_A$, $H_B$ and $H_C$ are maximum height attained by A, B and C respectively above inclined plane then: (Neglect air friction)
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity vector g acts vertically downwards.

Resolving g into components relative to inclined plane, the component acting perpendicular to and into the face of the incline is :

$$a_y'=-g\\cos \theta$$

Since all three stones are projected from the same incline, they all experience this same perpendicular deceleration component, $a_y'$.

Let a stone be projected with an initial speed $v_0$. If its angle of projection relative to the incline surface is $\beta$, then its initial velocity component perpendicular to the incline is:

$$u_y'=v_0\sin \beta$$

For the instantaneous perpendicular velocity using kinematic equation.

$$v_y'(t)=u_y'+a_y't=v_0\sin \beta-(g\cos \theta)t$$

The stone reaches its maximum height (H) above the inclined plane at the moment its perpendicular velocity component stops momentarily and becomes zero ($v_y'=0$).

Using third equation of motion, $v_y^2=u_y^2+2a_yy$

$$0^2=(v_0\sin \beta)^2-2g\cos \theta\,H$$

$$H=\frac{v_0^2\sin^2 \beta}{2g\cos \theta}$$

Stone A is thrown relative to the incline at $\alpha$. Thus, $\beta_A=\alpha$.

Stone B is projected with a right-angle to the incline. Thus, $\beta_B=90^\circ$.

Stone C is thrown at angle $90^\circ-\alpha$. Thus, $\beta_C=90^\circ-\alpha$.

It is given that all three stones are launched with equal initial speeds ($v_0$). Let the common constant factor $\frac{v_0^2}{2g\cos \theta}$ be represented by $k$.

For stone A

$$H_A = k\sin^2(\alpha)$$

For stone B

$$H_B = k\sin^2(90^\circ) = k(1)^2 = k$$

For stone C

$$H_C = k\sin^2(90^\circ - \alpha)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos\alpha$:

$$H_C = k\cos^2(\alpha)$$

Let us add the maximum heights of stone A and stone C together:

$$H_A + H_C = k\sin^2\alpha + k\cos^2\alpha$$

$$H_A + H_C = k(\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha = 1$:

$$H_A + H_C = k(1) = k$$

Since $H_B = k$,

$$H_A + H_C = H_B$$

Therefore, the correct relation is $H_A + H_C = H_B$. Thus, the correct option is A.

subject: Physics
q: An airplane pilot sets a compass course due west and maintains an air speed of 240 km/h. After flying $\frac{1}{2}$ h, he finds himself over a town that is 150 km west and 40 km south of his starting point. The wind velocity (with respect to ground) is :
question: An airplane pilot sets a compass course due west and maintains an air speed of 240 km/h. After flying $\frac{1}{2}$ h, he finds himself over a town that is 150 km west and 40 km south of his starting point. The wind velocity (with respect to ground) is :
solution:
The pilot making aircraft to head directly due West with a steady air speed of $240 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. This value is the speed of the plane relative to the surrounding air medium.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{p} / \mathrm{a}}=-2401 \hat{\mathrm{~km}} / \mathrm{h} $$

It is given that after flying for a time duration $\mathrm{t}=0.5$ hours $\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~h}\right)$, the plane's displacement vector relative to the ground starting point is:

$$ \vec{s}=(-150 \mathrm{i}-40 \mathrm{j}) \mathrm{km} $$

For the constant ground velocity vector is

$$ \vec{v}_g=\frac{\vec{s}}{t} $$

$$\vec{v}_g = \frac{-150\hat{i} - 40\hat{j}}{0.5}$$

$$\vec{v}_g = (-300\hat{i} - 80\hat{j})\ \text{km/h}$$

The relative velocity of plane with respect to air is,

$$\vec{v}_{p/a} = \vec{v}_g - \vec{v}_w$$

So, the velocity of wind $(\vec{v}_w)$ is, $\vec{v}_w = \vec{v}_g - \vec{v}_{p/a}$

$$\vec{v}_w = (-300\hat{i} - 80\hat{j}) - (-240\hat{i})$$

$$\vec{v}_w = (-300 + 240)\hat{i} - 80\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_w = (-60\hat{i} - 80\hat{j})\ \text{km/h}$$

The wind speed magnitude is,

$$|\vec{v}_w| = \sqrt{(-60)^2 + (-80)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 6400} = \sqrt{10000} = 100\ \mathrm{km/h}$$

The angle $\theta$ measured relative to the West direction line $(-\hat{i})$:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{80}{60} = \frac{4}{3}$$

The angle directly measured relative to the South axis $(-\hat{j})$:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{60}{80} = \frac{3}{4} \rightarrow \theta = 37^\circ$$

Therefore, the wind velocity with respect to the ground is $100\mathrm{km/h.},\ 37^\circ\ \mathrm{W\ of\ S}$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: An aeroplane has to travel from A to B in a straight path and then return to A along the same line. Its speed with respect to air (relative to wind) is V. A wind of speed v is blowing at right angles to the line AB. If the separation between A and B is l, then the total time taken for the complete to-and-fro journey is :
question: An aeroplane has to travel from A to B in a straight path and then return to A along the same line. Its speed with respect to air (relative to wind) is V. A wind of speed v is blowing at right angles to the line AB. If the separation between A and B is l, then the total time taken for the complete to-and-fro journey is :
solution:
The wind velocity vector relative to the ground is :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{w}}=\mathrm{vi} $$

The speed of the airplane relative to the wind is given as V .

To travel directly along the straight line from A to B , the net resultant velocity vector of the airplane relative to the ground $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{g} 1}\right)$ must point strictly along the positive x -axis with no lateral drift along the y -axis :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{g} 1}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{g} 1} \hat{1} $$

If velocity relative to the air medium $(\vec{v}_{p/w})$ and the wind velocity relative to the ground $(\vec{v}_{w})$,

$$\vec{v}_{p/w} = \vec{v}_{g1} - \vec{v}_{w}$$

$$\vec{v}_{p/w} = v_{g1}\hat{i} - v\hat{j}$$

The magnitudes to the airplane's airspeed $V$:

$$|\vec{v}_{p/w}|^2 = v_{g1}^2 + (-v)^2 \Rightarrow V^2 = v_{g1}^2 + v^2$$

$$v_{g1}^2 = V^2 - v^2 \Rightarrow v_{g1} = \sqrt{V^2 - v^2}$$

For uniform velocity let $t_1$ be the time taken to complete the forward journey of distance $l$:

$$ t_1=\frac{1}{v_{g 1}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{V^2-v^2}} $$

To return from B to A, the net ground velocity vector $\left(\vec{v}_{\mathbf{g} 2}\right)$ must point directly back along the negative x -axis:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{g} 2}=-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{g} 2} \hat{i} $$

The relative velocity of plane with relative to wind is:

$$\vec{v}_{p/w} = \vec{v}_{g2} - \vec{v}_w = -v_{g2}\hat{i} - v\hat{j}$$

$$\Rightarrow V^2 = (-v_{g2})^2 + (-v)^2 \Rightarrow V^2 = v_{g2}^2 + v^2$$

$$\Rightarrow v_{g2} = \sqrt{V^2 - v^2}$$

The time taken $(t_2)$ for the return trip of distance $l$ is:

$$t_2 = \frac{l}{v_{g2}} = \frac{l}{\sqrt{V^2 - v^2}}$$

The total flight time $(T)$ is

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{l}{\sqrt{V^2 - v^2}} + \frac{l}{\sqrt{V^2 - v^2}} = \frac{2l}{\sqrt{V^2 - v^2}}$$

Therefore, the total time for the round trip is $\frac{2l}{\sqrt{V^2 - v^2}}$ (Option A).

subject: Physics
q: For two particles A and B, given that $\vec{r}_A = 2 \hat{i} + 3 \hat{j}$, $\vec{r}_B = 6 \hat{i} + 7 \hat{j}$, $\vec{v}_A = 3 \hat{i} - \hat{j}$ and $\vec{v}_B = x \hat{i} - 5 \hat{j}$. What is the value of x if they collide:
question: For two particles A and B, given that $\vec{r}_A = 2 \hat{i} + 3 \hat{j}$, $\vec{r}_B = 6 \hat{i} + 7 \hat{j}$, $\vec{v}_A = 3 \hat{i} - \hat{j}$ and $\vec{v}_B = x \hat{i} - 5 \hat{j}$. What is the value of x if they collide:
solution:
For particle A the initial position $\vec{r}_{A0} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$ and the initial velocity is $\vec{v}_A = 3\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

For particle B the initial position $\vec{r}_{B0} = 6\hat{i} + 7\hat{j}$ and the initial velocity is $\vec{v}_B = x\hat{i} - 5\hat{j}$

Their position coordinates at any time $t$ are

$$\vec{r}(t) = \vec{r}_0 + \vec{v} \cdot t$$

Position vector of A at time $t$:

$$\vec{r}_A(t) = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) + (3\hat{i} - \hat{j})t = (2 + 3t)\hat{i} + (3 - t)\hat{j}$$

Position vector of B at time $t$:

$$\vec{r}_B(t) = (6\hat{i} + 7\hat{j}) + (x\hat{i} - 5\hat{j})t = (6 + xt)\hat{i} + (7 - 5t)\hat{j}$$

For two moving particles to collide, they must occupy the same coordinate position at the same instant of time.

If $t_c$ is the collision time, then:

$$\vec{r}_A(t_c) = \vec{r}_B(t_c)$$

$2 + 3t_c = 6 + xt_c$ and $3 - t_c = 7 - 5t_c$

$$3 - t_c = 7 - 5t_c$$

$$-t_c + 5t_c = 7 - 3$$

$$4t_c = 4$$

$$t_c = 1\ \text{second}$$

Substituting the value $t_c = 1\ \text{s}$ into the horizontal component:

$$2 + 3(1) = 6 + x(1)$$

$$2 + 3 = 6 + x$$

$$x = 5 - 6 = -1$$

Therefore, the value of $x$ is $-1$. Thus, option (B) is correct.

subject: Physics
q: Two particles A and B move with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$ respectively along the x and y axis. The initial separation between them is 'd' as shown in the figure. Find the least distance between them during their motion.
question: Two particles A and B move with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$ respectively along the x and y axis. The initial separation between them is 'd' as shown in the figure. Find the least distance between them during their motion.
solution:
Particle A starts at the origin. So, its initial position vector is:

$$\vec{r}_{A0} = 0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j}$$

It moves along the positive x-axis with a constant velocity $v_1$. Its velocity vector is:

$$\vec{v}_A = v_1\hat{i}$$

Particle B starts on the negative y-axis at a distance d below the origin. Its initial position vector is:

$$\vec{r}_{B0} = 0\hat{i} - d\hat{j}$$

It moves along the positive y-axis with a constant velocity $v_2$. Its velocity vector is:

$$\vec{v}_B = v_2\hat{j}$$

Since both particles move at constant velocities, their position vectors at any instantaneous time t are given by:

$$\vec{r}_A(t) = \vec{r}_{A0} + \vec{v}_A \cdot t = (v_1 t)\hat{i}$$

$$\vec{r}_B(t) = \vec{r}_{B0} + \vec{v}_B \cdot t = -d\hat{j} + (v_2 t)\hat{j} = (v_2 t - d)\hat{j}$$

The relative position vector of particle A with respect to particle B

$$\vec{r}_{A/B}(t) = \vec{r}_A(t) - \vec{r}_B(t)$$

$$\vec{r}_{A/B}(t) = (v_1 t)\hat{i} - (v_2 t - d)\hat{j} = (v_1 t)\hat{i} + (d - v_2 t)\hat{j}$$

The perpendicular distance (s) separating the two particles is the magnitude of this relative position vector.

$$s = \left|\vec{r}_{A/B}(t)\right| = \sqrt{(v_1 t)^2 + (d - v_2 t)^2}$$

$$s(t)=\sqrt{v_1^2 t^2 + (d^2 - 2dv_2 t + v_2^2 t^2)}$$

$$s(t)=\sqrt{(v_1^2 + v_2^2)t^2 - 2dv_2 t + d^2}$$

The function reaches its minimum when its first derivative with respect to time equals zero:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}s(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=0$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}s(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=2(v_1^2+v_2^2)t-2dv_2=0$$

$$2(v_1^2+v_2^2)t_{\min}-2dv_2=0$$

$$2(v_1^2+v_2^2)t_{\min}=2dv_2$$

$$t_{\min}=\frac{dv_2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}$$

Substituting this time value $t_{\min}$ into distance function $s(t)$:

$$s_{\min}^2=(v_1^2+v_2^2)\left(\frac{dv_2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}\right)^2-2dv_2\left(\frac{dv_2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}\right)+d^2$$

$$s_{\min}^2=\frac{d^2v_2^2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}-\frac{2d^2v_2^2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}+d^2$$

$$s_{\min}^2=d^2-\frac{d^2v_2^2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}$$

$$s_{\min}^2=\frac{d^2v_1^2+d^2v_2^2-d^2v_2^2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}$$

$$s_{\min}^2=\frac{d^2v_1^2}{v_1^2+v_2^2}$$

$$s_{\min}=\frac{dv_1}{\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}$$

The least distance between them during their motion is $\frac{dv_1}{\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: A rod of length L is sliding on a frictionless surface as shown in the figure. Velocity of end A is 4 m/s along the wall. Find the velocity of end B, when end B makes $30^o$ with wall PQ.
question: A rod of length L is sliding on a frictionless surface as shown in the figure. Velocity of end A is 4 m/s along the wall. Find the velocity of end B, when end B makes $30^o$ with wall PQ.
solution:
Let the intersection of the vertical wall PQ and the horizontal ground plane be the origin $\mathrm{Q}(0,0)$.

Let the position of end B moving down along the vertical wall be at a distance y from the origin $(0, \mathrm{y})$.

And the position of end A sliding along the horizontal surface be at a distance x from the origin, $(\mathrm{x}, 0)$.

The rod is rigid, so its total length L remains constant throughout the motion. At any given instant, the distances $x$, $y$, and the hypotenuse $L$ form a right-angled triangle.

Using Pythagorean theorem:

$$x^2 + y^2 = L^2$$

Since the rod is sliding, positions $x$ and $y$ are functions of time $(t)$, whereas length $L$ is a constant value $$\left(\frac{dL}{dt} = 0\right).$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(x^2) + \frac{d}{dt}(y^2) = \frac{d}{dt}(L^2)$$

$$2x\frac{dx}{dt} + 2y\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$$

The rate $\frac{dx}{dt}$ represents the horizontal velocity of end A moving away from the corner $(v_A = 4\ \text{m/s})$.

The rate $\frac{dy}{dt}$ represents the vertical velocity of end B moving downward toward the corner. Since $y$ is decreasing, $\frac{dy}{dt} = -v_B$, where $v_A = 4\ \text{m/s}$.

$$2x(v_A) + 2y(-v_B) = 0$$

$$x\,v_A = y\,v_B$$

$$v_B = \left(\frac{x}{y}\right) v_A$$

The rod makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the vertical wall PQ.

$$\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{x}{y} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting $\frac{x}{y} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and $v_A = 4\ \text{m/s}$

$$v_B = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times 4 = \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (D)

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are projected, not simultaneously, as shown in the same vertical plane. They collide one second after projecting the second one. The horizontal distance between them when the second one is projected is
question: Two projectiles are projected, not simultaneously, as shown in the same vertical plane. They collide one second after projecting the second one. The horizontal distance between them when the second one is projected is
solution:
Both projectiles are launched with the same speed $u$ and at the same angle $\theta$.

Horizontal velocity of projectile 1 is $v_{1x} = u \cos \theta$

Horizontal velocity of projectile 2 is $v_{2x} = u \cos \theta$

Since gravity only acts vertically, these horizontal velocities never change throughout the entire motion till they collide. Therefore, the relative horizontal velocity between them is:

$$v_{\mathrm{rel},x} = v_{1x} - v_{2x} = 0$$

For two particles to collide in a 2D plane, their horizontal positions must become equal at the moment of collision ($x_1 = x_2$). This means the final horizontal separation between them must change from its initial value to $0$.

As particle at the height $h$ is behind the particle at horizontal surface, so the first particle must be projected first to complete the initial horizontal separation $x$.

Let time taken for collision by the first particle is $T$ second and for the particle projected later is $1$ second.

Since their relative horizontal velocity is $0$, the horizontal distance between them can never increase or decrease while both are in the air. If they start with any horizontal separation when the second one is projected, that separation will remain unchanged for the next $1$ second. They will just fly parallel to each other horizontally.

Therefore, for them to collide $1$ second later (at $t = T - 1$) their horizontal separation must already be zero the moment the second particle is launched. They must be on the same vertical line.

Horizontal Distance $=0$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: Two beads A and B move along a semicircular wire frame as shown in figure. The beads are connected by an inelastic string which always remains tight. At an instant the speed of A is $u$, $\angle BAC = 45^{\circ}$ and $\angle BOC = 75^{\circ}$, where O is the centre of the semicircular arc. The speed of bead B at that instant is
question: Two beads A and B move along a semicircular wire frame as shown in figure. The beads are connected by an inelastic string which always remains tight. At an instant the speed of A is $u$, $\angle BAC = 45^{\circ}$ and $\angle BOC = 75^{\circ}$, where O is the centre of the semicircular arc. The speed of bead B at that instant is
solution:
The two beads are connected by an inelastic, inextensible string. For the string to remain tight without breaking or slacking, the component of velocity of bead A along the string line AB must equal the component of velocity of bead B along the string line AB.

$$v_{A\parallel} = v_{B\parallel}$$

$$v_A \cos \alpha = v_B \cos \beta \ldots (\mathrm{i})$$

Here $\alpha$ is the angle between the velocity of A and the string AB, and $\beta$ is the angle between the velocity of B and the string AB.

Since the beads are constrained to move on a circular wire track, the velocity vector of each bead is always directed tangentially to the circle at any point. A tangent to a circle is always at a right angle ($90^\circ$) to the radius drawn to that point.

Let O be the center of the circle.

Using vertically opposite angle for A, $\alpha = 45^\circ$

$\angle BOC = 75^{\circ}$.

Using exterior angle property for triangle OAB,

$$\angle BOC = \angle BAC + \angle OBA \rightarrow 75^{\circ} = 45^{\circ} + x$$

$$\Rightarrow x = 75^{\circ} - 45^{\circ}$$

$$\Rightarrow x = 30^{\circ}$$

$$\Rightarrow 90^{\circ} - \beta = 30^{\circ}$$

$$\Rightarrow \beta = 60^{\circ}$$

So, the angle between the velocity $\vec{v}_A$ and the line AB is $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$ and between the velocity $\vec{v}_B$ and the line AB is $\beta = 60^{\circ}$.

Applying the string constraint relation from equation (i):

$$u\cos(45^{\circ}) = v_B\cos(60^{\circ})$$

$$u \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = v_B \times \frac{1}{2}$$

$$v_B = u \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

Therefore, the speed of bead B at that instant is $\sqrt{2}u$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A point object of mass m is slipping down on a smooth hemispherical body of mass M & radius R. The point object is tied to a wall with ideal string as shown. At a certain instant the speed of hemisphere is v. Then speed v of the particle has value (neglect friction)
question: A point object of mass m is slipping down on a smooth hemispherical body of mass M & radius R. The point object is tied to a wall with ideal string as shown. At a certain instant the speed of hemisphere is v. Then speed v of the particle has value (neglect friction)
solution:
Let $x_{{H}}$ be the distance of the center of the hemisphere from the vertical wall. Since the hemisphere moves to the right with a speed $v$:

$$\frac{dx_{\text{H}}}{dt}=-v$$

The negative sign indicates that the distance to the wall is decreasing.

The total length of the ideal string $L$ is constant.

The length from the wall to the top of the hemisphere $l_1=x_{\text{H}}$.

The curved length of the string over the hemisphere from the top to the moving particle is $l_2$.

Therefore, the total length equation is:

$$L=l_1+l_2=x_{\text{H}}+l_2$$

Since the length of an ideal string is constant, its time derivative is zero:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{\mathrm{d}x_H}{\mathrm{d}t} + \frac{\mathrm{d}l_2}{\mathrm{d}t} = 0$$

Substituting $\frac{\mathrm{d}x_H}{\mathrm{d}t} = -v$:

$$-v + \frac{\mathrm{d}l_2}{\mathrm{d}t} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}l_2}{\mathrm{d}t} = v$$

Here, $\frac{\mathrm{d}l_2}{\mathrm{d}t}$ represents the speed of the particle relative to the hemisphereâ€™s top along its circular surface $v_{\mathrm{ph}}$.

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}l_2}{\mathrm{d}t} = v_{\mathrm{ph}} = v$$

So, the velocity of particle with respect to hemisphere is,

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{ph}} = -(v\sin 30^\circ)\hat{i} - (v\cos 30^\circ)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{ph}} = -\frac{v}{2}\hat{i} - \frac{v\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{j}$$

The velocity of hemisphere with respect to ground is $\vec{v}_{\mathrm{h}} = v\hat{i}$

If the velocity of the particle $\vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}}$ is the velocity of the hemisphere relative to ground then,

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{ph}} = \vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}} - \vec{v}_{\mathrm{h}}$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}} = \vec{v}_{\mathrm{h}} + \vec{v}_{\mathrm{ph}}$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}} = (v\hat{i}) + \left(-\frac{v}{2}\hat{i} - \frac{v\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{j}\right)$$

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}} = \left(v - \frac{v}{2}\right)\hat{i} - \left(\frac{v\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}} = \left(\frac{v}{2}\right)\hat{i} - \left(\frac{v\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\hat{j}$$

The speed is the magnitude of velocity of particle,

$$v_{\mathrm{p}} = \left|\vec{v}_{\mathrm{p}}\right| = \sqrt{\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)^2 + \left(-\frac{v\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{v^2}{4} + \frac{3v^2}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4v^2}{4}} = v$$

$$\Rightarrow v_{\mathrm{p}} = v$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: A man is running up the inclined plane (making an angle $\alpha$ from the horizontal) with a constant velocity. Rain is falling perpendicular to the incline plane. During motion man observes that rain is falling in vertical direction, when man doubles his velocity he observes that rain is making an angle $\alpha$ from the incline. Value of $\alpha$ is:
question: A man is running up the inclined plane (making an angle $\alpha$ from the horizontal) with a constant velocity. Rain is falling perpendicular to the incline plane. During motion man observes that rain is falling in vertical direction, when man doubles his velocity he observes that rain is making an angle $\alpha$ from the incline. Value of $\alpha$ is:
solution:
The incline makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal plane and the rain is falling perpendicular to the inclined plane.

$$\vec{V}_r = v_0(\sin \alpha\, \hat{i} - \cos \alpha\, \hat{j})$$

$$\vec{V}_r = v_0 \sin \alpha\, \hat{i} - v_0 \cos \alpha\, \hat{j}$$

Where $v_0$ is the unknown speed of the rain relative to the ground.

The man runs up the inclined plane with a constant velocity.

$$\vec{v}_m = v(\cos \alpha\, \hat{i} + \sin \alpha\, \hat{j})$$

$$\vec{v}_m = v \cos \alpha\, \hat{i} + v \sin \alpha\, \hat{j}$$

Where $v$ is the speed of the man.

For the first condition when initial velocity is $v$, the man observes that the rain falls in the vertical direction.

The velocity of the rain relative to the man is:

$$\vec{v}_{rm} = \vec{V}_r - \vec{v}_m$$

$$\vec{v}_{rm} = (v_0 \sin \alpha\, \hat{i} - v_0 \cos \alpha\, \hat{j}) - (v \cos \alpha\, \hat{i} + v \sin \alpha\, \hat{j})$$

$$= (v_0 \sin \alpha - v \cos \alpha)\hat{i} + (v_0 \cos \alpha - v \sin \alpha)\hat{j}$$

Since the man sees this relative velocity vector pointing down vertically, the x-component of the relative velocity must be zero.

$$v_0 \sin \alpha - v \cos \alpha = 0$$

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{v}{v_0} \Rightarrow v_0 = \frac{v}{\tan \alpha} = v \cot \alpha$$

$$\cot \alpha = \frac{v_0}{v} \ldots (i)$$

The man now doubles his velocity up the incline:

$$\vec{v}^{\,\prime}_m = 2v \cos \alpha \, \hat{i} + 2v \sin \alpha \, \hat{j}$$

The new velocity of the rain relative to the man is,

$$\vec{v}_{rm} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_m$$

$$\vec{v}_{rm} = \left( v_0 \sin \alpha \, \hat{i} - v_0 \cos \alpha \, \hat{j} \right) - \left( 2v \cos \alpha \, \hat{i} + 2v \sin \alpha \, \hat{j} \right)$$

$$= \left( v_0 \sin \alpha - 2v \cos \alpha \right) \hat{i} + \left( v_0 \cos \alpha - 2v \sin \alpha \right) \hat{j}$$

t the man now observes the rain making an angle $\alpha$ with the incline and the incline itself is making angle $37^\circ$ with horizontal. So, the rain is horizontal as observed by man, i.e, vertical component is zero.

$$\left( v_0 \cos \alpha - 2v \sin \alpha \right) = 0$$

$$\cot \alpha = \frac{2v}{v_0} \ldots (ii)$$

On multiplying equation (i) and equation (ii):

$$\cot^2 \alpha = \frac{v_0}{v} \times \frac{2v}{v_0}$$

$$\cot^2 \alpha = 2$$

$$\cot \alpha = \sqrt{2}$$

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$$

Therefore, the angle of inclination of inclined surface is $\tan^{-1} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: Two particles having position vectors $\vec{r}_1 = (3\hat{i} + 5\hat{j})$ metres and $\vec{r}_2 = (-5\hat{i} - 3\hat{j})$ metres are moving with velocities $\vec{v}_1 = (4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})$ m/s and $\vec{v}_2 = (\alpha \hat{i} + 7\hat{j})$ m/s. If they collide after 2 seconds, the value of â€˜$\alpha$â€™ is
question: Two particles having position vectors $\vec{r}_1 = (3\hat{i} + 5\hat{j})$ metres and $\vec{r}_2 = (-5\hat{i} - 3\hat{j})$ metres are moving with velocities $\vec{v}_1 = (4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})$ m/s and $\vec{v}_2 = (\alpha \hat{i} + 7\hat{j})$ m/s. If they collide after 2 seconds, the value of â€˜$\alpha$â€™ is
solution:
For two moving particles to collide at an instant in time, they must have same coordinates. Therefore, if they collide after a time interval $t = 2 ext{ s}$, their final position vectors at $t = 2 ext{ s}$ must be perfectly equal:

$$\vec{r}_{1f} = \vec{r}_{2f}$$

Since both velocity vectors $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$ are constant, the acceleration of both particles is zero.

$$\vec{v} = \frac{\Delta \vec{r}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\vec{r}_f - \vec{r}_i}{t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{r}_f = \vec{r}_i + \vec{v}t$$

So, the final position vectors at $t = 2\text{ s}$ for particle 1:

$$\vec{r}_{1f} = \vec{r}_{1i} + \vec{v}_1(2)$$

$$\vec{r}_{1f} = (3\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}) + (4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) \times 2$$

$$\vec{r}_{1f} = (3\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}) + (8\hat{i} + 6\hat{j})$$

$$\vec{r}_{1f} = (3 + 8)\hat{i} + (5 + 6)\hat{j} = 11\hat{i} + 11\hat{j}$$

The final position vectors at $t = 2\text{ s}$ for particle 2:

$$\vec{r}_{2f} = \vec{r}_{2i} + \vec{v}_2 \times (2)$$

$$\vec{r}_{2f} = (-5\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}) + (\alpha \hat{i} + 7\hat{j}) \times 2$$

$$\vec{r}_{2f} = (-5\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}) + (2\alpha \hat{i} + 14\hat{j})$$

$$\vec{r}_{2f} = (-5 + 2\alpha)\hat{i} + (-3 + 14)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{r}_{2f} = (2\alpha - 5)\hat{i} + 11\hat{j}$$

When they collide, $\vec{r}_{1f} = \vec{r}_{2f}$

$$11\hat{i} + 11\hat{j} = (2\alpha - 5)\hat{i} + 11\hat{j}$$

On equating the x-components,

$$11 = 2\alpha - 5$$

$$11 + 5 = 2\alpha$$

$$\alpha = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is 8. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: Consider a collection of large number of particles each moving with speed $v$. The direction of velocity is randomly distributed in the collection. The magnitude of relative velocity between a pair of particles averaged over all the pair is
question: Consider a collection of large number of particles each moving with speed $v$. The direction of velocity is randomly distributed in the collection. The magnitude of relative velocity between a pair of particles averaged over all the pair is
solution:
Consider two particles from the collection, particle 1 and particle 2 . Both move with the same speed v , but they are inclined at angle $\theta$.

Using the law of cosines for vector subtraction, the magnitude of the relative velocity vector $\vec{v}_{\text {rel }}=\vec{v}_1-\vec{v}_2$ is :

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = \sqrt{v^2 + v^2 - 2v^2 \cos \theta}$$

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = \sqrt{2v^2(1 - \cos \theta)}$$

Using the trigonometric half-angle identity $1 - \cos \theta = 2\sin^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$:

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = \sqrt{2v^2} \times 2\sin^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \sqrt{4v^2\sin^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}$$

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = 2v\sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Because the directions are randomly distributed in a three-dimensional collection, the probability of finding a pairing angle between $\theta$ and $\theta + d\theta$ is proportional to the surface area element of a sphere of unit radius, which is a ring of area $dS = 2\pi \sin \theta \, d\theta$.

The total surface area of a hemisphere containing all unique pairing angles from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = \pi$ is :

$$\int\limits_{0}^{\pi} 2\pi \sin \theta \, d\theta = 2\pi [-\cos \theta]_{0}^{\pi} = 2\pi (1 - (-1)) = 4\pi$$

Thus, the probability density function is $P(\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{2\pi \sin \theta \, d\theta}{4\pi} = \frac{1}{2} \sin \theta \, d\theta$.

The average value is calculated by integrating the product of the relative velocity magnitude and the probability weighting factor from 0 to $\pi$:

$$\langle v_{\mathrm{rel}} \rangle = \int\limits_{0}^{\pi} v_{\mathrm{rel}}(\theta) P(\theta) \, d\theta$$

$$\langle v_{\mathrm{rel}} \rangle = \int\limits_{0}^{\pi} \left[ 2v \sin\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \right] \left[ \frac{1}{2} \sin \theta \right] \, d\theta$$

Using the double-angle identity $\sin \theta = 2 \sin\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \cos\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right)$:

$$\langle v_{\mathrm{rel}} \rangle = v \int\limits_{0}^{\pi} \sin\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \left[ 2 \sin\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \cos\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \right] \, d\theta$$

$$\langle v_{\mathrm{rel}} \rangle = \int\limits_{0}^{\pi} \sin^2\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \cos\left( \frac{\theta}{2} \right) \, d\theta$$

Let $u = heta \sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$.

$$du = \frac{1}{2} \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\, d\theta \rightarrow 2du = \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\, d\theta.$$

When $\theta = 0 \Rightarrow u = \sin(0) = 0$

When $\theta = \pi \Rightarrow u = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1$

$$< v_{\mathrm{rel}} > = \int\limits_{0}^{1} 2v\, u^2\, (2du)$$

$$< v_{\mathrm{rel}} > = 4v \int\limits_{0}^{1} u^2\, du$$

$$< v_{\mathrm{rel}} > = 4v \left[\frac{u^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{1} = 4v\left(\frac{1}{3} - 0\right) = \frac{4}{3}v$$

$$< v_{\mathrm{rel}} > = \frac{4}{3}v \approx 1.33v > v$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: At a height 0.4 m from the ground, the velocity of a projectile in vector form is: $\vec{v} = (6 \hat{i} + 2 \hat{j})$ m/s. The angle of projection is $(g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2)$
question: At a height 0.4 m from the ground, the velocity of a projectile in vector form is: $\vec{v} = (6 \hat{i} + 2 \hat{j})$ m/s. The angle of projection is $(g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2)$
solution:
Let the point of projection on the ground be the origin $(0,0)$. At a height $y=0.4 \mathrm{~m}$, the instantaneous velocity vector is given as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=6 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}$.

Horizontal velocity component at this height: $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

Vertical velocity component at this height: $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

In projectile motion, there is no acceleration acting in the horizontal direction ($a_x = 0$). Therefore, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the flight.

Initial horizontal velocity $u_x = v_x = 6\ \text{m/s}$

In the vertical direction, the projectile experiences a constant downward acceleration due to gravity, $a_y = -g = -10\ \text{m/s}^2$.

Using the third equation of motion for the vertical component to find the initial vertical launch velocity ($u_y$):

$$v_y^2 = u_y^2 + 2a_y y$$

Substitute the known values $v_y = 2\ \text{m/s}$, $a_y = -10\ \text{m/s}^2$, and $y = 0.4\ \text{m}$ into the equation:

$$(2)^2 = u_y^2 + 2(-10)(0.4)$$

$$4 = u_y^2 - 8$$

$$u_y^2 = 4 + 8 = 12$$

$$u_y = \sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4 \times 3} = 2\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$$

The angle of projection $\theta$ is the angle that the initial velocity vector $\vec{u} = u_x\hat{i} + u_y\hat{j}$ makes with the horizontal ground plane.

$$\tan \theta = \frac{u_y}{u_x}$$

$$\tan \theta = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{6} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\theta = 30^\circ$$

Therefore, the angle of projection is $30^\circ$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: A river is flowing with a speed of $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. A swimmer wants to go to point $C$ starting from $A$. He swims with a speed of $\mathbf{5 ~ m} / \mathrm{s}$, at an angle $\theta$ w.r.t. river flow. If $A B=B C=\mathbf{1 0 0 0 ~ m}$. Then the value of $\theta$ in degree is
question: A river is flowing with a speed of $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. A swimmer wants to go to point $C$ starting from $A$. He swims with a speed of $\mathbf{5 ~ m} / \mathrm{s}$, at an angle $\theta$ w.r.t. river flow. If $A B=B C=\mathbf{1 0 0 0 ~ m}$. Then the value of $\theta$ in degree is
solution:
Let the starting point A be the origin $(0,0)$.

Since AB is perpendicular to the bank and has a width of 1000 m , the coordinates of $\mathrm{B}=(0,1000)$.

Point C is located a distance of 1000 m downstream from B . Therefore, the coordinates of $\mathrm{C}=(1000,1000)$.

The river flows horizontally to the right at a speed of 1 m/s.

$$\vec{v}_r = 1\hat{i} + 0\hat{j}$$

The swimmer moves at a speed of 5 m/s at an angle $\theta$ measured with respect to the direction of the river flow (the positive x-axis).

$$\vec{v}_{sr} = (5\cos\theta)\hat{i} + (5\sin\theta)\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity of swimmer ($\vec{v}_s$) with respect to river ($\vec{v}_r$) is $\vec{v}_{sr}$

$$\vec{v}_{sr} = \vec{v}_s - \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_s = \vec{v}_{sr} + \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_s = (5\cos\theta\,\hat{i} + 5\sin\theta\,\hat{j}) + (1\hat{i})$$

$$\vec{v}_s = (5\cos\theta + 1)\hat{i} + (5\sin\theta)\hat{j}$$

Horizontal velocity component is $v_x = 5\cos\theta + 1$

Vertical velocity component is $v_y = 5\sin\theta$

Let $t$ be the total time taken by the swimmer to travel from point A$(0,0)$ to point C$(1000,1000)$.

For the vertical motion while crossing the river of width AB:

Displacement along $y = v_y \, t$

$$1000 = (5\sin\theta)\, t \Rightarrow t = \frac{1000}{5\sin\theta} \ldots (i)$$

For the horizontal motion while drift downstream along BC:

Displacement along $x = v_x \, t$

$$1000 = (5\cos\theta + 1)\, t \Rightarrow t = \frac{1000}{5\cos\theta + 1} \ldots (ii)$$

Since the time $t$ spent crossing the river is equal to the time spent drifting downstream to reach point C,

$$\frac{1000}{5\sin\theta} = \frac{1000}{5\cos\theta + 1}$$

$$\frac{1}{5\sin\theta} = \frac{1}{5\cos\theta + 1}$$

$$5\cos\theta + 1 = 5\sin\theta$$

$$5\sin\theta - 5\cos\theta = 1$$

$$\sin\theta - \cos\theta = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

On squaring both sides of the equation:

$$(\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2 = (0.2)^2$$

$$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 0.04$$

Using the fundamental identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ and the double-angle identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$:

$$1 - \sin(2\theta) = 0.04$$

$$\sin(2\theta) = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = \frac{96}{100} = \frac{24}{25}$$

$$2\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) = \frac{24}{25}$$

$$\Rightarrow \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) = \frac{12}{25} = \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{4}{5}$$

If $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5}$ $\Rightarrow \theta = 37^\circ$

$$V_x = 5\cos\theta + 1 = 5\text{m/s}$$

$$V_y = 5\sin\theta = 4\,\text{m/s}$$

To have the same horizontal drift as vertical displacement $\theta = 37^\circ$ doesnâ€™t satisfy the condition.

If $\sin\theta = \frac{4}{5}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{3}{5}$ $\Rightarrow \theta = 53^\circ$

$$V_x = 5\cos\theta + 1 = 4\text{m/s}$$

$$V_y = 5\sin\theta = 4\,\text{m/s}$$

To have the same horizontal drift as vertical displacement $\theta = 53^\circ$ satisfies the condition.

Therefore, the value of $\theta$ in degrees is 53.

q: A body is projected horizontally from a point ' $A$ ' on the top of a tower and it reaches the ground after 3s at $B$. The straight line joining ' $A$ ' and ' $B$ ' is inclined at $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. If the horizontal speed at projection is $\mathrm{N} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$, determine $100N:\left(\right.$ take $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)($ Where $\sqrt{3}=1.73)$
question: A body is projected horizontally from a point ' $A$ ' on the top of a tower and it reaches the ground after 3s at $B$. The straight line joining ' $A$ ' and ' $B$ ' is inclined at $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. If the horizontal speed at projection is $\mathrm{N} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$, determine $100N:\left(\right.$ take $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)($ Where $\sqrt{3}=1.73)$
solution:
Let point A at the top of the tower be the origin $(0,0)$. The object is projected with an initial horizontal velocity $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{N} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and no initial vertical velocity $\left(\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=0\right)$.

Since there are no forces acting horizontally, the horizontal acceleration is zero ($a_x = 0$).

The total horizontal range (x):

$$x = u_x \, t = N \cdot 3 = 3N$$

The object experiences a constant downward acceleration due to gravity ($a_y = g = 10\,\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}^2}$).

The total vertical downward displacement (y) using equation of motion:

$$y = u_y t + \frac{1}{2} a_y t^2 = 0 \times 3 + \frac{1}{2} \times (-10) \times 3^2 = -\frac{1}{2}(10)(3)^2 = -5 \times 9 = -45\ \mathrm{m}$$

The negative sign shows the direction of point of strike with respect to initial point A.

The straight line connecting the launch point A to the landing point B makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal.

$$\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{\text{Vertical Displacement}}{\text{Horizontal Displacement}} = \frac{y}{x}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{45}{3N}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{15}{N}$$

$$N = 15\sqrt{3}$$

Putting the value of $\sqrt{3} = 1.73$,

$$N = 15 \times 1.73 = 25.95$$

Therefore, the value of 100N is 2595

q: A body is projected horizontally from the top of a tower with initial velocity $18 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. It hits the ground at angle $45^{\circ}$. If the vertical component of velocity when it strikes the ground is $\frac{5 \mathrm{~N}}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then find the value of $10N$ ?
question: A body is projected horizontally from the top of a tower with initial velocity $18 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. It hits the ground at angle $45^{\circ}$. If the vertical component of velocity when it strikes the ground is $\frac{5 \mathrm{~N}}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then find the value of $10N$ ?
solution:
When an object is launched horizontally from a tower, it undergoes horizontal projectile motion.

Let the horizontal direction is the x -axis and the vertical downward direction is the y -axis.

Since there are no external forces acting on the object horizontally (neglecting air resistance), the horizontal acceleration is zero ($a_x = 0$).

$$v_x(t) = \text{constant} = u_x$$

The initial horizontal projection speed is $u_x = 18\,\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}$, its horizontal velocity component remains completely unchanged throughout the entire flight up to the final moment it strikes the ground:

$$v_x = 18\,\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}$$

The object strikes the ground at an angle of $45^\circ$ relative to the horizontal surface.

At the point of impact, the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is composed of its two perpendicular vector components, $v_x$ (horizontal) and $v_y$ (vertical downward).

$$\tan(45^\circ) = \frac{v_y}{v_x}$$

$$1 = \frac{v_y}{v_x} \Rightarrow v_y = v_x$$

$$v_y = 18\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$$

$$V_y = 18\ \mathrm{m/s} = \frac{5N}{4}\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{5N}{4} = 18 \Rightarrow N = \frac{4 \times 18}{5} = 14.4$$

Therefore, the value of $10N$ is 144.

q: The horizontal and vertical distances travelled by a particle in time $t$ are given by $x=6 t$ and $y= 8 t-5 t^2$ If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2 ;$ then initial velocity of the particle is ______.
question: The horizontal and vertical distances travelled by a particle in time $t$ are given by $x=6 t$ and $y= 8 t-5 t^2$ If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2 ;$ then initial velocity of the particle is ______.
solution:
The position coordinates of the particle at any instant of time $t$ are given by equations:

$$x(t) = 6t$$

$$y(t) = 8t - 5t^2$$

The horizontal velocity component is,

$$v_x(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(6t) = 6\ \text{m/s}$$

The vertical velocity component is,

$$v_y(t) = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(8t - 5t^2)$$

$$v_y(t) = 8 - 10t$$

The initial velocity corresponds to time $t = 0$.

Initial horizontal velocity component $u_x = v_x(0) = 6\ \text{m/s}$

Initial vertical velocity component $u_y = v_y(0) = 8 - 10(0) = 8\ \text{m/s}$

The net magnitude of the initial velocity vector $(u)$

$$u = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2}$$

$$u = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10\ \text{m/s}$$

q:
A shell is fired from a gun from the bottom of a hill along its slope. The slope of the hill is $\alpha=30^{\circ}$, and the angle of this barrel to the horizontal is $\beta=60^{\circ}$. The Initial velocity $v$ of the shell is $21 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. The distance of point from the gun at which shell will fall is _______.

( Take : g = 9.8 m/s2 )
question:
A shell is fired from a gun from the bottom of a hill along its slope. The slope of the hill is $\alpha=30^{\circ}$, and the angle of this barrel to the horizontal is $\beta=60^{\circ}$. The Initial velocity $v$ of the shell is $21 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. The distance of point from the gun at which shell will fall is _______.

( Take : g = 9.8 m/s2 )
solution:
The gun's barrel is angled at $\beta=60^{\circ}$ relative to the horizontal, while the hill rises at an angle of $\alpha=30^{\circ}$ relative to the horizontal.

Therefore, the launch angle measured relative to the incline surface itself is:

$$\theta' = \beta - \alpha = 60^\circ - 30^\circ = 30^\circ$$

The initial speed $v = 21\ \text{m/s}$, so the component of initial velocity is;

$$u'_x = v\cos(\theta') = 21\cos(30^\circ) = 21 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{21\sqrt{3}}{2}\ \text{m/s}$$

$$u'_y = v\sin(\theta') = 21\sin(30^\circ) = 21 \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{21}{2}\ \text{m/s}$$

The acceleration due to gravity $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ acts vertically down.

$$a'_x = -g\sin(\alpha) = -g\sin(30^\circ) = -g \times \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{g}{2}$$

$$a'_y = -g\cos(\alpha) = -g\cos(30^\circ) = -g \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -\frac{g\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

The shell lands back on the slope of the hill when its net displacement perpendicular to the slope becomes zero

($y' = 0$).

Using equation of motion,

$$y'(T) = u_{y}'T + \frac{1}{2}a_{y}'T^2 = 0$$

$$T\left(u_{y}' + \frac{1}{2}a_{y}'T\right) = 0$$

$$u_{y}' + \frac{1}{2}a_{y}'T = 0 \rightarrow T = 2u_{y}' - a_{y}'$$

$$T = \frac{2 \times \left(\frac{21}{2}\right)}{\frac{g\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{42g}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The total distance along the incline where the shell hits the hill is its final $x'$-displacement at time $T$.

$$R = x'(T) = u_{x}'T + \frac{1}{2}a_{x}'T^2$$

$$R = \left(\frac{21\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{42g}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{g}{2}\right)\left(\frac{43g}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2$$

$$R = \frac{882}{g} - \frac{588}{g} = \frac{294}{g}\text{ meters}$$

$$R = 30\text{ meters}$$

q: A particle $P$ is projected from a point on the surface of smooth inclined plane (see figure). Simultaneously another particle $Q$ is released on the smooth inclined plane from the same position. $P$ and $Q$ collide after $t=4$ second. Then speed of projection of $P$ is :
question: A particle $P$ is projected from a point on the surface of smooth inclined plane (see figure). Simultaneously another particle $Q$ is released on the smooth inclined plane from the same position. $P$ and $Q$ collide after $t=4$ second. Then speed of projection of $P$ is :
solution:
The component of gravitational acceleration pulling them down along the slope surface :

$$a'_x = g \sin \theta$$

Particle Q is released from rest from the initial position. So, its initial velocity vector components are zero:

$$u_{x,Q} = 0, u_{y,Q} = 0$$

Its position coordinate along the downward slope after any time $t$ is $x^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} a t^2$ :

$$ \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{Q}}^{\prime}(\mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~g} \sin \theta) \mathrm{t}^2 $$

$$y'_Q(t)=0$$

Particle P is projected with an initial velocity $u$ at some angle $\alpha$ relative to the inclined surface.

Its initial velocity components are,

$$u_{x,P}=u\cos\alpha$$

$$u_{y,P}=u\sin\alpha$$

It has the same downward acceleration $a'_x=g\sin\theta$.

Horizontal position of particle P relative to inclined plane is,

$$x'_P(t)=(u\cos\alpha)t+\frac{1}{2}(g\sin\theta)t^2$$

Vertical position of particle P to the incline is,

$$y'_P(t)=(u\sin\alpha)t-\frac{1}{2}(g\cos\theta)t^2$$

The two particles are to collide at a time $t=4\text{ s}$. For a collision to happen, their coordinates must be same at that moment:

$x'_P(4)=x'_Q(4)$ and $y'_P(4)=y'_Q(4)$

$$ (u\cos\alpha)t+\frac{1}{2}(g\sin\theta)t^2=\frac{1}{2}(g\sin\theta)t^2 $$

$$(u\cos\alpha)t=0$$

Since the collision time is $t=4\text{ s}\rightarrow t\ne 0$ and the projection speed $u\ne 0$,

$$\cos\alpha=0\Rightarrow \alpha=90^\circ$$

So, particle P must have been projected perpendicular to the inclined plane surface.

Using y-coordinates, $y'_P=y'_Q=0$ at $t=4\text{ s}$ using $\alpha=90^\circ$:

$$(u\sin 90^{\circ})t - \frac{1}{2}(g\cos \theta)t^2 = 0$$

$$u \cdot t = \frac{1}{2}(g\cos \theta)t^2$$

$$u = \frac{1}{2}(g\cos \theta)t$$

$$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times \cos(60^{\circ}) \times 4$$

$$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 12 \times 4 = 10\ \mathrm{m/s}$$
